question: As illustrated in the provided diagram of pentagonal tiling, why is a fivefold rotation axis ($2\pi/5$) prohibited in an infinite periodic crystal lattice?

options.value: Pentagons can only form a lattice if the primitive translation vectors $\\mathbf{a}_1$ and $\\mathbf{a}_2$ are unequal.
A periodic lattice must be invariant under translations $\\mathbf{T}$, and fivefold symmetry operations cannot fill a 2D plane without leaving gaps or creating overlaps.
Fivefold symmetry is only possible in three-dimensional Bravais lattices like the face-centered cubic (fcc) system.
The Wigner-Seitz cell of a fivefold symmetric structure would have an infinite volume, violating the definition of a primitive cell.

explanation: According to the crystallographic restriction theorem, only 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6-fold rotation axes are compatible with translational symmetry. As shown in the figure, attempting to tile a plane with pentagons (fivefold symmetry) results in gaps or overlaps, meaning it cannot form a connected, periodic array that fills all space.

question: Referring to the symmetry axes of a cube shown in the attached image, which of the following correctly describes the 'triad axes' (3-fold rotation axes) of the cubic system?

options.value: There are three triad axes passing through the centers of opposite faces.
There are six triad axes passing through the midpoints of opposite edges.
There are four triad axes, each passing through opposite vertices (body diagonals) of the cube.
There is only one triad axis, which is perpendicular to the diagonal plane of symmetry.

explanation: In a cubic lattice, there are four triad axes. Each axis corresponds to a body diagonal of the cube (connecting opposite vertices). A rotation of $2\pi/3$ ($120^\\circ$) about any of these four axes carries the cube into itself. This is shown in part (d) of the provided symmetry diagram.
